Illustrative Example 2 - Confidence Intervals for the Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

Ivan Jacob Agaloos Pesigan

Source: vignettes/fig-example-2.Rmd
fig-example-2.Rmd

This vignette accompanies Illustrative Example 2. The goal of the example is to calculate confidence intervals for the direct, indirect, and total effects from the continuous-time vector autoregressive model drift matrix 𝚽\boldsymbol{\Phi} for a range of time intervals. This example features the DeltaMed, MCMed, BootMed, DeltaMedStd, MCMedStd, and BootMedStd functions from the cTMed package.

library(dynr)
library(simStateSpace)
library(cTMed)

Continuous-Time Vector Autoregressive Model Estimates

The object fit contains the fitted dynr model for the data set with a sample size of 133. See Fitting the Continuous-Time Vector Autoregressive Model for more details.

Extract Elements of the Drift Matrix and the Process Noise Covariance Matrix

We extract the elements of the drift matrix and the process noise covariance matrix and their corresponding sampling covariance matrix from the fit object.

# drift matrix
phi_names <- c(
  "phi_11",
  "phi_21",
  "phi_31",
  "phi_12",
  "phi_22",
  "phi_32",
  "phi_13",
  "phi_23",
  "phi_33"
)
phi <- matrix(
  data = coef(fit)[phi_names],
  nrow = 3
)
# column names and row names are needed to define the indirect effects
colnames(phi) <- rownames(phi) <- c(
  "conflict",
  "knowledge",
  "competence"
)
vcov_phi_vec <- vcov(fit)[phi_names, phi_names]
# process noise covariance matrix
sigma_names <- c(
  "sigma_11",
  "sigma_12",
  "sigma_13",
  "sigma_12",
  "sigma_22",
  "sigma_23",
  "sigma_13",
  "sigma_23",
  "sigma_33"
)
sigma <- matrix(
  data = coef(fit)[sigma_names],
  nrow = 3
)
# measurement error covariance matrix
theta_names <- c(
  "theta_11",
  "theta_22",
  "theta_33"
)
theta <- diag(3)
diag(theta) <- coef(fit)[theta_names]
# combining the drift matrix and the process noise covariance matrix
phi_sigma_names <- c(
  "phi_11",
  "phi_21",
  "phi_31",
  "phi_12",
  "phi_22",
  "phi_32",
  "phi_13",
  "phi_23",
  "phi_33",
  "sigma_11",
  "sigma_12",
  "sigma_13",
  "sigma_22",
  "sigma_23",
  "sigma_33"
)
vcov_theta <- matrix(
  data = vcov(fit)[phi_sigma_names, phi_sigma_names],
  nrow = length(phi_sigma_names)
)

Delta Method Confidence Intervals For The Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

Using the DeltaMed function from the cTMed package, confidence intervals for the direct, indirect, and total effects for a time interval of one, two, and three are given below.

DeltaMed(
  phi = phi,
  vcov_phi_vec = vcov_phi_vec,
  delta_t = c(1, 2, 3),
  from = "conflict",
  to = "competence",
  med = "knowledge"
)
#> 
#> Total, Direct, and Indirect Effects
#> 
#> $`1`
#>          interval     est     se       z      p    2.5%  97.5%
#> total           1 -0.0828 0.0428 -1.9360 0.0529 -0.1667 0.0010
#> direct          1 -0.0771 0.0437 -1.7643 0.0777 -0.1627 0.0085
#> indirect        1 -0.0057 0.0061 -0.9416 0.3464 -0.0177 0.0062
#> 
#> $`2`
#>          interval     est     se       z      p    2.5%   97.5%
#> total           2 -0.1373 0.0670 -2.0497 0.0404 -0.2685 -0.0060
#> direct          2 -0.1207 0.0695 -1.7371 0.0824 -0.2568  0.0155
#> indirect        2 -0.0166 0.0173 -0.9612 0.3365 -0.0504  0.0172
#> 
#> $`3`
#>          interval     est     se       z      p    2.5%   97.5%
#> total           3 -0.1716 0.0809 -2.1210 0.0339 -0.3302 -0.0130
#> direct          3 -0.1443 0.0848 -1.7025 0.0887 -0.3105  0.0218
#> indirect        3 -0.0273 0.0281 -0.9712 0.3314 -0.0822  0.0277

Monte Carlo Method Confidence Intervals For The Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

Using the MCMed function from the cTMed package, confidence intervals for the direct, indirect, and total effects for a time interval of one, two, and three are given below.

MCMed(
  phi = phi,
  vcov_phi_vec = vcov_phi_vec,
  delta_t = c(1, 2, 3),
  from = "conflict",
  to = "competence",
  med = "knowledge",
  R = 20000L,
  seed = 42
)
#> 
#> Total, Direct, and Indirect Effects
#> 
#> $`1`
#>          interval     est     se     R    2.5%  97.5%
#> total           1 -0.0828 0.0429 20000 -0.1669 0.0012
#> direct          1 -0.0771 0.0438 20000 -0.1650 0.0081
#> indirect        1 -0.0057 0.0064 20000 -0.0194 0.0060
#> 
#> $`2`
#>          interval     est     se     R    2.5%   97.5%
#> total           2 -0.1373 0.0679 20000 -0.2717 -0.0037
#> direct          2 -0.1207 0.0704 20000 -0.2666  0.0124
#> indirect        2 -0.0166 0.0181 20000 -0.0536  0.0193
#> 
#> $`3`
#>          interval     est     se     R    2.5%   97.5%
#> total           3 -0.1716 0.0832 20000 -0.3395 -0.0087
#> direct          3 -0.1443 0.0873 20000 -0.3338  0.0148
#> indirect        3 -0.0273 0.0298 20000 -0.0860  0.0351

Parametric Bootstrap Method Confidence Intervals For The Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

The parametric bootstrap approach involves generating data from the parametric model and fitting the model on the generated data multipke times. The data generation and model fitting is hadled by the simStateSpace package. The estimated parameters are passed as arguments to the PBSSMOUFixed function from the simStateSpace package, which generates a parametric bootstrap sampling distribution of the parameter estimates. The argument R specifies the number of bootstrap replications. The generated data and model estimates are be stored in path using the specified prefix for the file names. The ncores = parallel::detectCores() argument instructs the function to use all available CPU cores in the system.

NOTE: Fitting the CT-VAR model multiple times is computationally intensive.

R <- 1000L
path <- root$find_file(
  ".setup",
  "data-raw"
)
prefix <- "pb_example_133"
phi_hat <- phi
sigma_hat <- sigma
nu <- c(0, 0, 0)
lambda <- diag(3)
# fixed mu0 and sigma0
data("grundy2007", package = "manCTMed")
data_0 <- grundy2007[which(grundy2007[, "time"] == 0), ]
varnames <- c(
  "conflict",
  "knowledge",
  "competence"
)
mu0 <- colMeans(data_0)[varnames]
sigma0 <- cov(data_0)[varnames, varnames]
boot <- PBSSMOUFixed(
  R = R,
  path = path,
  prefix = prefix,
  n = 133,
  time = 30,
  delta_t = 0.10,
  mu0 = mu0,
  sigma0_l = t(chol(sigma0)),
  mu = c(0, 0, 0),
  phi = phi,
  sigma_l = t(chol(sigma)),
  nu = nu,
  lambda = lambda,
  theta_l = t(chol(theta)),
  mu0_fixed = TRUE,
  sigma0_fixed = TRUE,
  ncores = parallel::detectCores(),
  seed = 42
)

Using the BootMed function from the cTMed package, confidence intervals for the direct, indirect, and total effects for a time interval of one, two, and three are given below.

ci <- BootMed(
  phi = extract(object = boot, what = "phi"), # extracts the bootstrap drift matrices
  phi_hat = phi_hat,
  delta_t = c(1, 2, 3),
  from = "conflict",
  to = "competence",
  med = "knowledge"
)
summary(ci)
#>     effect interval          est          se    R        2.5%         97.5%
#> 1    total        1 -0.082830099 0.038839149 1000 -0.15857211 -0.0067447338
#> 2   direct        1 -0.077087636 0.039136185 1000 -0.15279503  0.0002762973
#> 3 indirect        1 -0.005742463 0.004132077 1000 -0.01486750  0.0009337155
#> 4    total        2 -0.137257463 0.061181585 1000 -0.25798543 -0.0166954224
#> 5   direct        2 -0.120663647 0.061572293 1000 -0.23958489  0.0004530425
#> 6 indirect        2 -0.016593816 0.011795758 1000 -0.04200704  0.0027314095
#> 7    total        3 -0.171598190 0.074225990 1000 -0.32394299 -0.0243010211
#> 8   direct        3 -0.144345904 0.074293526 1000 -0.29040288  0.0005657691
#> 9 indirect        3 -0.027252286 0.019357469 1000 -0.06865166  0.0053818978
summary(ci, type = "bc") # bias-corrected
#>     effect interval          est          se    R        2.5%         97.5%
#> 1    total        1 -0.082830099 0.038839149 1000 -0.15827071 -0.0067284469
#> 2   direct        1 -0.077087636 0.039136185 1000 -0.15279503  0.0002762973
#> 3 indirect        1 -0.005742463 0.004132077 1000 -0.01677540  0.0001650497
#> 4    total        2 -0.137257463 0.061181585 1000 -0.25773872 -0.0164675508
#> 5   direct        2 -0.120663647 0.061572293 1000 -0.24341285 -0.0006077631
#> 6 indirect        2 -0.016593816 0.011795758 1000 -0.04670110  0.0008068482
#> 7    total        3 -0.171598190 0.074225990 1000 -0.32412418 -0.0248197412
#> 8   direct        3 -0.144345904 0.074293526 1000 -0.29542857 -0.0026213182
#> 9 indirect        3 -0.027252286 0.019357469 1000 -0.07560494  0.0024325605

Delta Method Confidence Intervals For The Standardized Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

Using the DeltaMedStd function from the cTMed package, confidence intervals for the standardized direct, indirect, and total effects for a time interval of one, two, and three are given below.

DeltaMedStd(
  phi = phi,
  sigma = sigma,
  vcov_theta = vcov_theta,
  delta_t = c(1, 2, 3),
  from = "conflict",
  to = "competence",
  med = "knowledge"
)
#> 
#> Total, Direct, and Indirect Effects
#> 
#> $`1`
#>          interval     est     se       z      p    2.5%  97.5%
#> total           1 -0.0956 0.0558 -1.7133 0.0867 -0.2051 0.0138
#> direct          1 -0.0890 0.0557 -1.5987 0.1099 -0.1981 0.0201
#> indirect        1 -0.0066 0.0074 -0.8933 0.3717 -0.0212 0.0079
#> 
#> $`2`
#>          interval     est     se       z      p    2.5%  97.5%
#> total           2 -0.1585 0.0881 -1.7986 0.0721 -0.3312 0.0142
#> direct          2 -0.1393 0.0876 -1.5901 0.1118 -0.3111 0.0324
#> indirect        2 -0.0192 0.0210 -0.9112 0.3622 -0.0604 0.0221
#> 
#> $`3`
#>          interval     est     se       z      p    2.5%  97.5%
#> total           3 -0.1982 0.1067 -1.8567 0.0634 -0.4073 0.0110
#> direct          3 -0.1667 0.1058 -1.5747 0.1153 -0.3741 0.0408
#> indirect        3 -0.0315 0.0342 -0.9212 0.3570 -0.0984 0.0355

Monte Carlo Method Confidence Intervals For The Standardized Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

Using the MCMedStd function from the cTMed package, confidence intervals for the standardized direct, indirect, and total effects for a time interval of one, two, and three are given below.

MCMedStd(
  phi = phi,
  sigma = sigma,
  vcov_theta = vcov_theta,
  delta_t = c(1, 2, 3),
  from = "conflict",
  to = "competence",
  med = "knowledge",
  R = 20000L,
  seed = 42
)
#> 
#> Total, Direct, and Indirect Effects
#> 
#> $`1`
#>          interval     est     se     R    2.5%  97.5%
#> total           1 -0.0956 0.0677 20000 -0.2333 0.0036
#> direct          1 -0.0890 0.0666 20000 -0.2260 0.0104
#> indirect        1 -0.0066 0.0086 20000 -0.0262 0.0066
#> 
#> $`2`
#>          interval     est     se     R    2.5%   97.5%
#> total           2 -0.1585 0.1080 20000 -0.3811 -0.0013
#> direct          2 -0.1393 0.1052 20000 -0.3584  0.0164
#> indirect        2 -0.0192 0.0243 20000 -0.0728  0.0208
#> 
#> $`3`
#>          interval     est     se     R    2.5%   97.5%
#> total           3 -0.1982 0.1325 20000 -0.4760 -0.0065
#> direct          3 -0.1667 0.1284 20000 -0.4423  0.0197
#> indirect        3 -0.0315 0.0396 20000 -0.1170  0.0377

Parametric Bootstrap Method Confidence Intervals For The Standardized Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

Using the MCMedStd function from the cTMed package, confidence intervals for the standardized direct, indirect, and total effects for a time interval of one, two, and three are given below.

pb <- BootMedStd(
  phi = extract(object = boot, what = "phi"), # extracts the bootstrap drift matrices
  sigma = extract(object = boot, what = "sigma"), # extracts the bootstrap process noise covariance matrices
  phi_hat = phi_hat,
  sigma_hat = sigma_hat,
  delta_t = c(1, 2, 3),
  from = "conflict",
  to = "competence",
  med = "knowledge"
)
summary(pb)
#>     effect interval          est          se   R        2.5%         97.5%
#> 1    total        1 -0.095646850 0.050188622 999 -0.20368381 -0.0067932439
#> 2   direct        1 -0.089015825 0.049719287 999 -0.19241664  0.0002108455
#> 3 indirect        1 -0.006631025 0.005158217 999 -0.01882737  0.0010841985
#> 4    total        2 -0.158496057 0.079867920 999 -0.33781326 -0.0176140363
#> 5   direct        2 -0.139334589 0.077972907 999 -0.30524055  0.0003450431
#> 6 indirect        2 -0.019161468 0.014738021 999 -0.05317883  0.0035171688
#> 7    total        3 -0.198150511 0.097330287 999 -0.41728092 -0.0244380601
#> 8   direct        3 -0.166681330 0.093594630 999 -0.36956048  0.0004302193
#> 9 indirect        3 -0.031469181 0.024165354 999 -0.08734549  0.0064530805
summary(pb, type = "bc") # bias-corrected
#>     effect interval          est          se   R        2.5%         97.5%
#> 1    total        1 -0.095646850 0.050188622 999 -0.20737779 -0.0091184523
#> 2   direct        1 -0.089015825 0.049719287 999 -0.19746337 -0.0011981283
#> 3 indirect        1 -0.006631025 0.005158217 999 -0.02055410  0.0001106158
#> 4    total        2 -0.158496057 0.079867920 999 -0.34685496 -0.0216382718
#> 5   direct        2 -0.139334589 0.077972907 999 -0.31385489 -0.0027954931
#> 6 indirect        2 -0.019161468 0.014738021 999 -0.05900613  0.0007659007
#> 7    total        3 -0.198150511 0.097330287 999 -0.42909365 -0.0311469829
#> 8   direct        3 -0.166681330 0.093594630 999 -0.37484786 -0.0030606408
#> 9 indirect        3 -0.031469181 0.024165354 999 -0.09535630  0.0019420703
